title: Why Do People Resist Change Even When It Benefits Them?

hook.text: You've rolled out something objectively better. A new tool, a simpler process. The logic is airtight. And yet — people drag their feet. They find reasons it won't work. Some actively sabotage.

story_beats.text: Five monkeys in a cage. A banana at the top of a ladder.
Every time one climbs up, all five get sprayed with cold water.
Soon, no monkey climbs. Then one monkey is replaced with a new one.
The new monkey reaches for the banana. The others pull it down. Hard.
One by one, every original monkey is replaced. None of the current monkeys have ever been sprayed.
But none will climb. And none know why. 'That's how it's always been done here.'

challenges.situation: Your team won't adopt the new process. You've shown them the data three times. Nothing moves.

options.text: Show even more data — a side-by-side comparison with numbers
options.feedback: You're arguing with emotion using reason. Like explaining the chemistry of fire to someone whose house is burning. The resistance isn't logical — it's emotional.

options.text: Say: 'I know this means re-learning something you're already good at. That's real.'
options.feedback: Naming the loss opens the door. People need to feel that their discomfort is seen, not dismissed.

options.text: Escalate — get leadership to mandate the switch
options.feedback: Forced change confirms their fear. It hardens resistance into identity: 'I'm someone who doesn't go along with management's whims.'

challenges.situation: You acknowledged the loss. They seem more open. Next step?

options.text: Announce full migration starting next week
options.feedback: Too big, too fast. You lowered defenses — then triggered the identity threat again. The first step needs to be tiny.

options.text: Ask one volunteer to try it for one small task this week
options.feedback: Small enough to stay below the threat threshold. One person succeeding becomes proof for everyone else. People trust peers more than presentations.

options.text: Run a 2-hour training session for everyone
options.feedback: Training assumes the problem is knowledge. But they're not confused — they're afraid. A training won't address that.

challenges.situation: One person tried it and loved it. But the team expert in the old system feels threatened — their status was built on mastering it.

options.text: Tell them the old system is going away whether they like it or not
options.feedback: You just confirmed their worst fear — their expertise is worthless now. They'll become your loudest opponent.

options.text: Ignore their concerns — focus on the people who are adopting
options.feedback: They're influential. Ignoring them means they'll quietly undermine the change. Status threat doesn't go away by being ignored.

options.text: Make them the guide for the new system — their expertise gets a new home
options.feedback: Protect status during transition. Their competence isn't deleted — it's redirected. Now they're the expert in something new, not a beginner.

challenges.situation: In the 1990s, Kodak engineers built one of the first digital cameras. Leadership buried it. The company filed for bankruptcy in 2012.
challenges.question: Which force primarily drove the resistance?

options.feedback: Fear played a role, but the deeper issue was that everyone — executives to factory workers — had built their identity around film.
Exactly. Digital wasn't just a product — it was a threat to who they were. Their expertise, daily routines, and sense of self were all built on film.
The incentives actually pointed toward digital (that's where the market was going). But identity overrode rational incentives.

principle.text: People don't resist change. They resist loss.
principle.subtext: Every change asks someone to give up familiarity, competence, or status. The brain weighs losses roughly twice as heavily as equivalent gains.

reflection.question: What change am I currently resisting — and what's the loss I'm actually afraid of?
